九月文化

The Ninth Moon

Led by a Grammy-winning musician, poet, tea master, and holistic healer who is impossibly Chinese and impossibly American. Not tours. Fellowships.

Cultural immersions in China & Hong Kong

In Chinese tradition, each lunar month carries its own moon — its own mood, its own longing. The eighth moon is the Harvest Moon, a time of reunion and fullness.

But the Ninth Moon is different. It falls when the rice is cut and the chrysanthemums bloom. In the poetry of Li Bai and Du Fu, it is the moon of travelers — of those who belong to two worlds. It is the moon of looking back at home while walking toward the unfamiliar. Of knowing you are exactly between things.

That is what we offer. Not a tour of China. A temporary membership in the in-between — guided by someone who was born in Hong Kong, has lived in America for twenty-five years, and now moves between Foshan and Portland.

The throughline

Every journey is built on the same foundation: these are my friends. The tea farmers, the calligrapher, the chefs, the hotel owners. You are not only being taken to attractions. You are being introduced to people — real people, in their real lives — who will become part of yours. Your only job is to arrive permeable. To let joy and wonder and strangeness come in and wake something up again. Leave the rest to me.

A signature offering
☕ Pastry-and-Learn

Every morning of every journey begins the same way: pastry, tea, and Emily. Each session is an original talk — history, culture, food, people, stories — tailored to exactly where we are and what we're about to experience. It gives you the lens before you need it. By the time we walk out the door, you're not a tourist. You're someone who understands what they're looking at.

Why travel with me

China is one of the most visited countries on earth. And yet for many Westerners, it remains out of reach — not because of distance, but because of the feeling that it is too complex, too foreign, too politically charged to simply go.

I have spent my life on both sides of that gap. And what I know is this: China is history you can taste. Food that has been perfected over a thousand years. Art that exists nowhere else. Nature that will make you quiet. Stories in every street, every bowl, every face.

The complexity is real. The rewards are greater. You just need someone who can hold both at once — and introduce you to the place, not the idea of it.

Every person I have taken to China comes back different. Not dramatically — quietly. Better framing. More world. The assumptions loosen. The hate gets harder to sustain when you've eaten in someone's home and laughed with their grandmother.

This is my love letter to the world. I have a rare thing — a foot in each place, fluency in both directions — and I want to use it. To open friendships and possibilities that make a little more room for joy.

Come with me.

Before every journey, I do a comprehensive check-in with each traveler — your concerns, your body, your curiosity, your limits. I group people deliberately, not randomly. I think carefully about who will bring out the best in whom. Most people return from these trips as dear friends. That part is not an accident.

On language
You don't need Chinese. Cities are enormous, friendly, and well-connected. Get WeChat, Alipay, an eSIM, and a translation app. Learn a few phrases if you'd like — or more, if you're inspired. People love it either way. And also: I take care of you.
On being noticed
In Hong Kong you blend in easily. In mainland China you may be stared at — and so will the pretty person next to you, and the old man across the street, and the baby in the carrier. It's a live-and-let-live culture in the most intoxicating sense. You can sing loudly, dance wildly, wear an insane costume in public at any hour — and no one will judge you. China is full of life and possibility in a way that is genuinely rare in the world. Take the stares as curiosity. They usually are.
On air quality
This is not the China of twenty years ago. Most cars are electric. Air quality in Shunde, Chengdu, Lijiang, and Hong Kong is generally excellent. Second-hand smoke exists in places — but clean air does not require a mask.
On safety
These are mega-cities with 20 million people, public transport everywhere, and police who will genuinely help you if needed. You cannot get lost. This is my home turf — I know the rules, the back doors, and who to call.
On money and prices
Most people are just living their lives — China is not out to get you. For big purchases, take my advice. I know real prices and real people.
On politics and speech
Tourists talk openly and freely. What you may find is that certain questions Americans carry — about Tibet, about Xinjiang — land with confusion rather than alarm. Not because people can't answer, but because the framing feels foreign to daily life. Regular people are living their lives. They are not monitoring yours. This is equally true in Hong Kong, where people talk loudly and freely about everything — including how much the middle-aged generation can't stand mainlanders.
On feeling at home
You are not on a bus with 40 strangers. You are my guest. My aunt, my brother, my mom, and my high school friends will be amongst the smiley faces that greet you.
On what I bring
Trilingual fluency — English, Mandarin, Cantonese, and the unspoken stuff in between. Deep friendships with artists, chefs, farmers, cultural bearers, hotel owners. A lifetime of knowing this food — cooking it, ordering it, understanding why it matters. I love the people, and they love me back. You feel it the moment we arrive somewhere together. Some days we plan. Some days we follow what's alive. There are no stupid questions.

You're not buying a tour.
You're buying time inside a world most people never find — because they didn't know who to ask.

Longevity retreats
📅 By request
⏱ 21 days

A month in Yunnan and Foshan, immersed in traditional Chinese health practices. Unprecedented personal support and guidance throughout.

Body reset
📅 By request
⏱ 7 nights
👥 3–5 persons

Massages, facials, body treatments, daily movement, and a world-class hospital body check package — all in Foshan, one of the best places on earth for exactly this. Add delicious meals and a Traditional Chinese Medicine consultation. A full reset, from the outside in. Leave lighter and clearer than you arrived.

How it works

Each journey begins with a theme, a season, and a small group of curious humans — carefully matched. The itinerary is a framework, not a script. Every day is shaped by weather, energy, and what's alive in the room.

The product is not the hotel room (although we always stay in rather beautiful ones). It is access to a world most people never find — because they didn't know who to ask.

Get in touch

Inquiries, questions, or just curious — write to singingemily@gmail.com
